In C++, the code can be written as:

#include <vector>
#include <set>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int flipLights(int n, int presses) {
        if (presses == 0) return 1;
        set<vector<bool>> states;
        // Iterate all 16 possible combinations
        for (int mask = 0; mask < 16; ++mask) {
            int b1 = (mask >> 3) & 1;
            int b2 = (mask >> 2) & 1;
            int b3 = (mask >> 1) & 1;
            int b4 = mask & 1;
            int sum = b1 + b2 + b3 + b4;
            if ((sum % 2) != (presses % 2)) continue;
            vector<bool> state(n, true); // initially all on
            for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
                int flips = b1;
                if (i % 2 == 0) flips += b2;
                if (i % 2 == 1) flips += b3;
                if ((i - 1) % 3 == 0) flips += b4;
                state[i-1] = (flips % 2 == 0);
            }
            states.insert(state);
        }
        return states.size();
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <set>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int flipLights(int n, int presses) {
        if (presses == 0) return 1;
        set<vector<bool>> states;
        for (int mask = 0; mask < 16; ++mask) {
            int b1 = (mask >> 3) & 1;
            int b2 = (mask >> 2) & 1;
            int b3 = (mask >> 1) & 1;
            int b4 = mask & 1;
            int sum = b1 + b2 + b3 + b4;
            if ((sum % 2) != (presses % 2)) continue;
            vector<bool> state(n, true);
            for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
                int flips = b1;
                if (i % 2 == 0) flips += b2;
                if (i % 2 == 1) flips += b3;
                if ((i - 1) % 3 == 0) flips += b4;
                state[i-1] = (flips % 2 == 0);
            }
            states.insert(state);
        }
        return states.size();
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1) (since the loop runs a fixed 16 times regardless of input size)
space complexity: O(n) (for storing the state vectors)
energy implications: LOW (the solution is computationally efficient and doesn't require significant resources)
explanation: The solution efficiently explores all possible combinations of button presses by considering each button's parity (pressed odd or even times). It computes the resulting bulb states for each valid combination and counts the unique states, ensuring optimal performance even for the upper constraint limits.
